title: Column lineage for ducklake pipelines
text: ’ll build lake two related tables. already one, skip ahead pipe queries. Load customers orders, commit messages lake’s audit trail says table exists:
code: library(dplyneage) library(ducklake) library(dplyr)  install_ducklake()  lake_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "lineage_lake") dir.create(lake_dir, showWarnings = FALSE) attach_ducklake("demo_lake", lake_path = lake_dir) customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = 1:5,   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana", "Eve"),   region = c("east", "west", "east", "south", "west") )  orders <- data.frame(   order_id = 1:10,   customer_id = rep(1:5, each = 2),   amount = c(100, 150, 200, 75, 300, 125, 180, 90, 250, 160) )  with_transaction(   create_table(customers, "customers"),   author = "Data Engineer",   commit_message = "Load customer master" ) #> Transaction started. #> Transaction committed.  with_transaction(   create_table(orders, "orders"),   author = "Data Engineer",   commit_message = "Load order transactions" ) #> Transaction started. #> Transaction committed.

title: Column lineage for ducklake pipelines
text: typical analysis query — join orders customers, aggregate region — piped straight lineage extraction: Two things worth noticing: Lake tables appear blue source nodes plain names (orders, customers) — names passed create_table(), catalog prefix cluttering diagram. Python involved. ducklake queries dbplyr pipelines hood, dplyneage’s pure-R engine reads column provenance directly query tree. total_sales attributes orders.amount region customers.region, exactly, nothing install beyond two packages.
code: get_ducklake_table("orders") |>   left_join(get_ducklake_table("customers"), by = "customer_id") |>   group_by(region) |>   summarise(total_sales = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "400px")

what: Layered lakes: one diagram per hop
title: Column lineage for ducklake pipelines
text: ducklake encourages layered (bronze/silver/gold) architecture layer materialized create_table(). Lineage slots naturally workflow: pipeline builds layer also recipe lineage diagram, can document hop moment materialize . downstream query now sees region_sales source, lineage diagram documents just hop:
code: region_sales <- get_ducklake_table("orders") |>   left_join(get_ducklake_table("customers"), by = "customer_id") |>   group_by(region) |>   summarise(total_sales = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")  with_transaction(   create_table(region_sales, "region_sales"),   author = "Data Analyst",   commit_message = "Gold layer: sales by region" ) #> Transaction started. #> Transaction committed. top_regions <- get_ducklake_table("region_sales") |>   filter(total_sales > 300)  top_regions |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

what: The whole lake in one diagram
title: Column lineage for ducklake pipelines
text: Per-hop diagrams useful moment build layer; named list stitches full picture. Pass layer’s build pipeline name materialized , extract_lineage() connects hops: source table matching model name becomes intermediate transform node. Now orders customers (blue) feed region_sales (orange), feeds top_regions (green) — whole lake, bronze gold, every column edge preserved. Aggregation edges carry defining expression label, total_sales reads sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE) right diagram. stitched graph also impact analysis pays . lineage_upstream() walks hops transitively: rename orders.amount touches every layer lake — worth knowing commit .
code: lake_lineage <- extract_lineage(list(   region_sales = region_sales,   top_regions = top_regions ))  lineage_flow(lake_lineage, height = "450px") lineage_upstream(lake_lineage, "top_regions.total_sales") #> [1] "orders.amount"            "region_sales.total_sales"

title: Column lineage for ducklake pipelines
text: ducklake’s snapshots version data; lineage describes pipeline’s structure. compose: can extract lineage time-travel query just like . One implementation detail surfaces . get_ducklake_table_version() builds query raw SQL ((VERSION => ...) hood), pure-R engine can’t see inside — extract_lineage() falls back sqlglot engine, message. sqlglot parses DuckDB’s time-travel syntax traces columns identically; practical difference path uses dplyneage’s Python dependency (provisioned automatically — see vignette("python-integration")). diagram every version, ’s point: snapshots change data , columns come . refactor ever change provenance, lineage_json() gives stable document can commit diff CI — see vignette("getting-started").
code: first_version <- list_table_snapshots("orders")$snapshot_id[[1]]  get_ducklake_table_version("orders", first_version) |>   group_by(customer_id) |>   summarise(total = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px") #> Falling back to the sqlglot engine: The pure-R lineage engine does not support tables defined by raw SQL (`tbl(con, sql(...))`).

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: dplyr/dbplyr pipelines analyzed entirely R, nothing else install. Raw SQL strings analyzed sqlglot, dplyneage’s one Python dependency — install reticulate package enable engine, sqlglot provisioned automatically first time ’s needed. See vignette("python-integration") manage Python environment.
code: pak::pak("tgerke/dplyneage")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: Let’s create small -memory DuckDB database work : simplest lineage — two columns selected one table: output column connects back source column came . Try dragging tables around, zooming mouse wheel, hovering column highlight connections.
code: library(dplyneage) library(dplyr) library(duckdb)  con <- dbConnect(duckdb(), ":memory:")  customers <- tibble(   id = 1:5,   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana", "Eve"),   email = paste0(tolower(name), "@example.com") )  orders <- tibble(   order_id = 1:10,   customer_id = rep(1:5, each = 2),   amount = c(100, 150, 200, 75, 300, 125, 180, 90, 250, 160) )  copy_to(con, customers, "customers", overwrite = TRUE) copy_to(con, orders, "orders", overwrite = TRUE) tbl(con, "customers") |>   select(id, name) |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: Lineage becomes useful transformations pile . join followed aggregation: Notice total_spent traces back orders.amount — customers, even though amount appears unqualified generated SQL. pass dbplyr table, dplyneage doesn’t parse SQL : walks pipeline’s query tree, records exactly table column came , attribution always right. pipeline embeds raw SQL dbplyr::sql(), query tree can’t see inside string, extract_lineage() hands whole query sqlglot instead (message). can also force specific engine engine = "r" engine = "sqlglot" — see ?extract_lineage.
code: tbl(con, "customers") |>   left_join(tbl(con, "orders"), by = c("id" = "customer_id")) |>   group_by(id, name) |>   summarise(total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "400px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: cases break naive lineage tools. dplyneage handles engines resolve full query structure rather pattern-matching column names: dbplyr pipelines query tree, raw SQL sqlglot’s lineage engine. ’ll use raw SQL keep examples compact.

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: Columns traced intermediate CTEs back base tables — recent transparent, amount correctly attributes orders:
code: extract_lineage("   WITH recent AS (     SELECT customer_id, amount FROM orders WHERE order_date > '2024-01-01'   )   SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM recent GROUP BY customer_id ") |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: computed column can come several tables . COALESCE full join gets edge sources: applies arithmetic across tables (o.amount * r.rate), CASE expressions, branches UNION. dbplyr pipelines get treatment: full_join() key column traces sides, union_all() column every branch.
code: extract_lineage("   SELECT COALESCE(u.email, a.email) AS email   FROM users u FULL JOIN archive a ON u.id = a.id ") |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: schema available, SELECT * expands real columns. dbplyr input never needs schema — pipeline knows columns — raw SQL, pass one :
code: extract_lineage(   "SELECT * FROM customers",   schema = list(customers = c("id", "name", "email")) ) |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: examples show, extract_lineage() accepts SQL string directly — useful auditing queries didn’t write R. Two things know: Qualified columns (o.amount) always resolve correctly, schema . Unqualified columns need schema attributed certainty. Pass named list mapping table columns: Without schema, SELECT * expanded produces warning rather silently empty diagram. Queries SQL dialects work setting dialect:
code: extract_lineage(   "SELECT c.name, order_date    FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id",   schema = list(     customers = c("id", "name", "email"),     orders = c("order_id", "customer_id", "order_date", "amount")   ) ) |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "postgres") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "snowflake")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: extract_lineage() reads lineage lazy query — tree dbplyr builds anything touches database. pipeline plain tibble tree: dplyr executes verb immediately, time ask lineage, result left. workaround one line. dbplyr::memdb_frame() puts data throwaway -memory SQLite database (install RSQLite package ) hands back lazy table, identical pipeline becomes traceable: data frame already , copy_to(dbplyr::memdb(), df, name = "df") copy. Lineage depends pipeline’s structure, never data, large frames copying slice enough — copy_to(dbplyr::memdb(), head(df), name = "df") yields diagram copying every row. duckdb connection earlier vignette works just well (copy_to(con, df)); memdb_frame() simply fastest route connection exists yet.
code: sales <- dbplyr::memdb_frame(   customer_id = c(1, 1, 2),   amount = c(100, 250, 40),   .name = "sales" )  sales |>   group_by(customer_id) |>   summarise(total = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE)) |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: documentation design sketches, skip extraction entirely build diagram : Nodes come three types — "source" (blue), "transform" (orange), "target" (green) — edges accept label (e.g. "SUM()") animated = TRUE emphasis. lineage_example() renders complete hand-built diagram can use template.
code: nodes <- list(   create_table_node(     table_name = "customers",     columns = c("id", "name", "email"),     x = 0, y = 100,     table_type = "source"   ),   create_table_node(     table_name = "customer_summary",     columns = c("customer_id", "full_name", "contact"),     x = 500, y = 100,     table_type = "target"   ) )  edges <- list(   create_column_edge("customers", "id", "customer_summary", "customer_id"),   create_column_edge("customers", "name", "customer_summary", "full_name"),   create_column_edge("customers", "email", "customer_summary", "contact") )  lineage_flow(nodes, edges, height = "300px")

title: Getting Started with dplyneage
text: Diagrams answer questions interactively; sometimes need lineage plain data. Two exporters cover common cases. lineage_json() serializes nodes, edges, metadata small, stable JSON document. output deterministic, can commit alongside pipeline code let CI diff : refactor silently changes column comes , diff shows ships. also natural handoff format data catalogs anything scriptable jq. lineage_graphml() writes GraphML, XML format graph tools speak: igraph, Gephi, yEd open directly. Every column becomes node, makes real graph queries possible. classic one impact analysis — “orders.amount changes, outputs affected?” — reverse, tracing output back every source column feeds : functions return serialized string called without path, compose pipes tests.
code:
lineage <- tbl(con, "customers") |>   left_join(tbl(con, "orders"), by = c("id" = "customer_id")) |>   group_by(id, name) |>   summarise(total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage()  lineage_json(lineage) #> { #>   "metadata": { #>     "sql": "SELECT id, "name", SUM(amount) AS total_spent
FROM (
  SELECT customers.*, order_id, amount
  FROM customers
  LEFT JOIN orders
    ON (customers.id = orders.customer_id)
) AS q01
GROUP BY id, "name"", #>     "dialect": "duckdb", #>     "engine": "r", #>     "node_count": 3, #>     "edge_count": 3 #>   }, #>   "nodes": [ #>     { #>       "id": "customers", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["id", "name"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "orders", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["amount"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "output", #>       "type": "target", #>       "columns": ["id", "name", "total_spent"] #>     } #>   ], #>   "edges": [ #>     { #>       "source": "customers", #>       "source_column": "id", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "id", #>       "transformation": "identity", #>       "expression": "id" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "customers", #>       "source_column": "name", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "name", #>       "transformation": "identity", #>       "expression": "name" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "amount", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "total_spent", #>       "transformation": "aggregation", #>       "expression": "sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE)" #>     } #>   ] #> } path <- tempfile(fileext = ".graphml") lineage_graphml(lineage, path)  g <- igraph::read_graph(path, format = "graphml")  # Everything upstream of total_spent igraph::subcomponent(g, "output.total_spent", mode = "in") #> + 2/6 vertices, named, from f916e50: #> [1] output.total_spent orders.amount  # Everything downstream of orders.amount igraph::subcomponent(g, "orders.amount", mode = "out") #> + 2/6 vertices, named, from f916e50: #> [1] orders.amount      output.total_spent

what: Installation: There Is No Step Two
title: Python Integration
text: reticulate installed, Python setup automatic. first time lineage extraction needs sqlglot, reticulate : Find suitable Python (downloading self-contained build via uv none configured) Provision ephemeral virtual environment containing sqlglot Cache everything, subsequent sessions start quickly can verify availability time: Note: install_sqlglot() earlier development versions deprecated nothing — manual installation step anymore.
code: library(dplyneage)  # This just works - no install step required extract_lineage("SELECT id, name FROM customers") |>   lineage_flow() has_sqlglot() #> [1] TRUE

title: Python Integration
text: manage Python environment (project virtualenv, conda env, system Python), reticulate respect usual. Just make sure sqlglot installed : See ?reticulate::use_virtualenv reticulate Python version docs ways select environment.
code: pip install 'sqlglot>=23.0.0' # Point reticulate at your environment before loading dplyneage Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = "/path/to/your/python")  library(dplyneage) has_sqlglot()

title: Python Integration
text: engines emit lineage metadata, everything downstream shared. one runs controlled engine argument extract_lineage(): "auto" (default) picks R engine lazy tables sqlglot SQL strings, falling back sqlglot pipeline uses something R engine trace. metadata$engine result records one ran.
code: dbplyr pipeline ──→ pure-R walk of the lazy query tree                                   │ raw SQL string ──→ Python (sqlglot.lineage engine)                                   ↓               Column Lineage Metadata (per output column)                                   ↓                     R (create nodes & edges)                                   ↓                  React Flow Visualization

title: Python Integration
text: Walks dbplyr’s lazy query tree, reading exact column provenance selects, mutates, joins, set operations — SQL parsing, Python .onLoad(): declares sqlglot requirement via py_require() imports bundled Python module delay_load (Python start first use) has_sqlglot(): checks availability Built sqlglot.lineage.lineage(), handles scope resolution, aliases, CTE trace-, set operations, star expansion extract_lineage(): traces output column source columns list_tables(): enumerates base tables (used schema harvesting) extract_lineage(): main user-facing function, dispatches engine harvest_schema(): reads table schemas database connection unqualified columns attributed right table convert_lineage_to_graph(): creates visualization nodes edges

title: Python Integration
text: SQL alone always say table unqualified column belongs . dbplyr lazy tables sidestep problem entirely: R engine reads provenance query tree, schema ever needed. (lazy table falls back sqlglot, dplyneage lists columns referenced table live connection hands schema sqlglot automatically.) raw SQL strings, can pass schema : Without schema, fully qualified columns still resolve correctly; unqualified ones may traceable, SELECT * expanded (’ll get warning).
code: extract_lineage(   "SELECT c.name, order_date FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id",   schema = list(     customers = c("id", "name"),     orders = c("customer_id", "order_date")   ) )

title: Python Integration
text: sqlglot supports many SQL dialects. Specify dialect extracting lineage: dialect match database backend ensure accurate parsing. See sqlglot documentation full list dialects.
code: extract_lineage(query, dialect = "duckdb")     # default extract_lineage(query, dialect = "postgres") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "snowflake") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "bigquery") extract_lineage(query, dialect = "mysql")

title: Python Integration
text: dbplyr pipelines: Python startup cost — R engine runs immediately First raw-SQL call: may take moment Python environment initializes (, first run, provisions) Subsequent calls: fast (<100ms typical queries) Complex queries: sqlglot handles CTEs, subqueries, window functions, complex joins, set operations (UNION, INTERSECT, etc.)

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: dplyneage draws interactive column-level lineage diagrams dplyr dbplyr pipelines. Pipe query extract_lineage() traces every output column back source columns came — joins, aggregations, CTEs, unions, computed expressions — renders result draggable, zoomable React Flow diagram lineage_flow(). dbplyr pipelines analyzed pure R walking lazy query tree, Python involved. Raw SQL goes sqlglot’s dedicated lineage engine instead, means many dialects (DuckDB, PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, …) work .

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: Build dplyr pipeline database usual, pipe extract_lineage() lineage_flow():  Behind one pipe, extract_lineage(): walks pipeline’s lazy query tree pure R, tracing every output column source columns (joins, aggregations, unions, multi-source computed columns resolve exactly) falls back sqlglot’s lineage engine pipeline injects raw SQL dbplyr::sql(), pass SQL string directly (path handles aliases, CTEs, subqueries, reads table schemas connection unqualified columns attribute correctly) resulting diagram fully interactive: drag tables rearrange, zoom pan, hover columns highlight connections. Computed columns carry defining expression edge label, aggregation edges animate.
code: library(dplyneage) library(dplyr) library(dbplyr) library(duckdb)  con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), ":memory:")  customers <- tibble(   customer_id = 1:5,   first_name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana", "Eve"),   last_name = c("Smith", "Jones", "Brown", "Wilson", "Davis"),   email = paste0(tolower(first_name), "@example.com") )  orders <- tibble(   order_id = 1:10,   customer_id = rep(1:5, each = 2),   amount = c(100, 150, 200, 75, 300, 125, 180, 90, 250, 160) )  copy_to(con, customers, "customers", overwrite = TRUE) copy_to(con, orders, "orders", overwrite = TRUE)  tbl(con, "customers") |>   select(customer_id, first_name, last_name, email) |>   left_join(tbl(con, "orders"), by = "customer_id") |>   group_by(customer_id, first_name, last_name, email) |>   summarise(     total_orders = n_distinct(order_id),     total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   ) |>   extract_lineage() |>   lineage_flow(height = "600px")

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: Real pipelines materialize layers — bronze tables feed silver summary, silver feeds gold. Pass extract_lineage() named list, one element per layer, stitches single DAG: source table whose name matches another element’s name links model’s node.  Intermediate models render orange transform nodes, terminal models green targets, impact questions now span whole pipeline:
code: silver <- tbl(con, "orders") |>   group_by(customer_id) |>   summarise(total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") invisible(compute(silver, name = "silver", temporary = TRUE))  gold <- tbl(con, "silver") |>   mutate(big_spender = total_spent > 400)  extract_lineage(list(silver = silver, gold = gold)) |>   lineage_flow(height = "450px") #> file:////private/var/folders/fw/0d9nr9951q57f0d5l6qc1j200000gn/T/RtmpF7IxQF/file177c06a1c37a7/widget177c06289eb5d.html screenshot completed extract_lineage(list(silver = silver, gold = gold)) |>   lineage_upstream("gold.big_spender") #> [1] "orders.amount"      "silver.total_spent"

title: Column Lineage Visualization for dplyr Pipelines
text: Diagrams people; lineage also useful plain data. lineage_edges() flattens one classified row per column edge, lineage_upstream() / lineage_downstream() answer impact questions directly: lineage_diff() compares two extractions — run across branches CI provenance changes surface ship. interchange, lineage_json() gives small, stable document can query jq, feed data catalog, commit next pipeline code: Written file, document scriptable outside R entirely — ’s jq answering “source columns feed total_spent?”: lineage_graphml() writes GraphML, opens directly graph tools like Gephi, yEd, igraph. question works graph query — scales transitive ancestry pipelines chain:
code:
lineage <- tbl(con, "orders") |>   left_join(tbl(con, "customers"), by = "customer_id") |>   group_by(customer_id, first_name) |>   summarise(total_spent = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>   extract_lineage()  lineage_edges(lineage) #>   source_table source_column target_table target_column transformation #> 1       orders   customer_id       output   customer_id       identity #> 2    customers    first_name       output    first_name       identity #> 3       orders        amount       output   total_spent    aggregation #>                  expression #> 1               customer_id #> 2                first_name #> 3 sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE)  lineage_upstream(lineage, "output.total_spent") #> [1] "orders.amount" lineage_json(lineage) #> { #>   "metadata": { #>     "sql": "SELECT customer_id, first_name, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
FROM (
  SELECT orders.*, first_name, last_name, email
  FROM orders
  LEFT JOIN customers
    ON (orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id)
) AS q01
GROUP BY customer_id, first_name", #>     "dialect": "duckdb", #>     "engine": "r", #>     "node_count": 3, #>     "edge_count": 3 #>   }, #>   "nodes": [ #>     { #>       "id": "orders", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["customer_id", "amount"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "customers", #>       "type": "source", #>       "columns": ["first_name"] #>     }, #>     { #>       "id": "output", #>       "type": "target", #>       "columns": ["customer_id", "first_name", "total_spent"] #>     } #>   ], #>   "edges": [ #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "customer_id", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "customer_id", #>       "transformation": "identity", #>       "expression": "customer_id" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "customers", #>       "source_column": "first_name", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "first_name", #>       "transformation": "identity", #>       "expression": "first_name" #>     }, #>     { #>       "source": "orders", #>       "source_column": "amount", #>       "target": "output", #>       "target_column": "total_spent", #>       "transformation": "aggregation", #>       "expression": "sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE)" #>     } #>   ] #> } lineage_json(lineage, "lineage.json") jq -r '.edges[] | select(.target_column == "total_spent")        | "\(.source).\(.source_column)"' lineage.json #> orders.amount path <- tempfile(fileext = ".graphml") lineage_graphml(lineage, path)  g <- igraph::read_graph(path, format = "graphml") igraph::subcomponent(g, "output.total_spent", mode = "in") #> + 2/6 vertices, named, from dc9ae91: #> [1] output.total_spent orders.amount

title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
text: sql dbplyr lazy table (tbl_lazy), single SQL query string, named list (one element per pipeline model; see Details). Lazy tables analyzed directly lazy query tree (SQL recorded metadata still comes dbplyr::sql_render()); one handled sqlglot engine instead, database connection used harvest table schemas automatically. Plain data frames accepted — dplyr executes verb immediately, leaving query tree read. Wrap data dbplyr::memdb_frame() (copy existing frame copy_to(dbplyr::memdb(), df, name = "df")) pipeline becomes traceable; see vignette("getting-started"). dialect SQL dialect query written , e.g. "duckdb" (default), "postgres", "mysql", "snowflake", "bigquery". dialect sqlglot understands works . schema Optional table schema used sqlglot engine attribute unqualified columns right table expand SELECT *: named list mapping table names character vectors column names, e.g. list(orders = c("order_id", "amount")). relevant SQL strings — R engine reads exact provenance lazy query tree, lazy table falls back sqlglot harvests schema database connection automatically. show_sql TRUE, print SQL analyzed. Useful seeing dbplyr generated pipeline. Default: FALSE. engine lineage engine use. "auto" (default) uses pure-R engine lazy tables dbplyr (>= 2.5.0) installed, falling back sqlglot SQL strings unsupported constructs. "r" forces pure-R engine errors anything trace. "sqlglot" always renders SQL analyzes sqlglot. include_indirect TRUE, columns used filter()/, join conditions, group_by(), arrange()/ORDER also appear diagram, connected dashed edges (see Details). Default: FALSE, matching lineage tools.

title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
text: list nodes edges ready pass lineage_flow(), plus metadata recording analyzed SQL, dialect, engine used, node/edge counts.

title: Extract column lineage from a dplyr pipeline or SQL query — extract_lineage
text: Two engines available. dbplyr lazy tables analyzed pure-R fast path walks pipeline's lazy query tree directly — Python required. SQL strings analyzed sqlglot's lineage engine via reticulate (Suggests dependency: install reticulate enable engine; sqlglot provisioned automatically). pipeline uses construct R engine trace (e.g. raw SQL injected dbplyr::sql()), falls back sqlglot automatically. engines trace select-list lineage default: columns used filter(), join conditions, arrange() create lineage edges. Set include_indirect = TRUE add dashed edges — column filters result still breaks pipeline dropped, impact analysis usually wants . Indirect edges connect filter/join/group/sort column every output column, since conditions shape whole result, classified column used ("filter", "join", "group_by", "sort"). named list stitches multi-model pipeline one graph. element (lazy table SQL string) analyzed , source table whose name matches another element's name connects model's node — bronze/silver/gold flow layer materialized model's name renders single multi-hop DAG, intermediate models drawn orange transform nodes terminal models green targets.

title: dplyneage (development version)
text: extract_lineage() now gives actionable error passed plain data frame, pointing dbplyr::memdb_frame() / copy_to() workaround instead failing later misleading message Python SQL strings. workaround also documented README ?extract_lineage. New lineage_openlineage() exports lineage OpenLineage RunEvent ColumnLineage facets — interchange format Marquez, DataHub, OpenMetadata ingest, dplyneage-extracted lineage can sit alongside lineage dbt, Airflow, Spark. Edge classifications map OpenLineage transformation types, including INDIRECT subtypes include_indirect edges. New lineage_mermaid() exports lineage Mermaid flowchart — paste ```mermaid fence renders natively GitHub, Quarto, documentation tools, htmlwidget involved. Tables draw colored subgraphs, non-identity edges carry expression, indirect edges draw dashed. getting-started vignette now covers local data frames: plain-dplyr pipelines lazy query tree trace, dbplyr::memdb_frame() (copy_to()) one-line workaround makes identical pipeline traceable. New include_indirect argument extract_lineage(): columns used filter()/, join conditions, group_by(), arrange()/ORDER — shape result without appearing — draw dashed edges output column, classified use ("filter", "join", "group_by", "sort"). Impact analysis via lineage_upstream()/lineage_downstream() sees : dropping column used filter() still breaks pipeline. engines support , multi-model pipelines stitch indirect edges across layers. extract_lineage() now stitches multi-model pipelines: pass named list lazy tables SQL strings (one element per model) source table matching another element’s name links model’s node, bronze/silver/gold flow renders one multi-hop DAG. Intermediate models draw orange transform nodes, terminal models green targets, metadata$models records model’s SQL engine. Diagrams laid height-aware layered algorithm: pipeline hop advances one column, nodes stack spacing accounts column count (tall tables longer overlap), layers vertically centered. ducklake vignette now ends stitched whole-lake diagram transitive lineage_upstream() impact query. extract_lineage() results now classed dplyneage_lineage compact print method summarising engine, tables, output columns, edge count. New lineage_edges() lineage_tables() flatten lineage object plain data frames — one classified row per column edge, one row per table.
